items.title: NeMLA Panel 2027: Women's Writing and the Question of Canon
items.excerpt: Women's writing emerged as a critical category in response to the historical exclusion of women from literary canons and archives. The recovery of neglected texts, authors, and traditions has transformed literary studies, creating new genealogies of reading and interpretation. Yet the category itself raises important questions. Does the designation "women...
items.fullDescription: Women's writing emerged as a critical category in response to the historical exclusion of women from literary canons and archives. The recovery of neglected texts, authors, and traditions has transformed literary studies, creating new genealogies of reading and interpretation. Yet the category itself raises important questions. Does the designation "women's writing" continue to serve as a productive framework, or does it risk reinforcing the very marginalization it seeks to address by positioning women outside the literary mainstream? As Elaine Showalter argues, feminist criticism should not simply "fit women between the lines of the male tradition" but attend to the cultural and historical conditions that shape women's literary production and reception. Her influential formulation of gynocriticism intends to recover neglected women writers and establish women's writing as a significant field of scholarly inquiry. Subsequent feminist scholarship has complicated these foundations further. Black feminist critics such as Barbara Smith, bell hooks, Hortense Spillers challenge the tendency to treat "woman" as a universal category, while postcolonial theorists including Gayatri Chakravorty Spivak and Chandra Talpade Mohanty question the assumption of a coherent global female experience. Likewise, theorists such as Judith Butler and Jack Halberstam have interrogated the stability of gender itself. Together, these interventions have encouraged scholars to frame women's writing not as a singular tradition but as a diverse and contested field shaped by race, class, caste, sexuality, nationality, language, and other intersecting forms of identity and power. This panel invites papers that reconsider critical strategies for studying women's writing, with particular attention to the reception of women's writing. How do questions of race, class, sexuality, language, nation, and geography influence the ways women writers are read, taught, circulated, translated, archived, and canonized? Topics may include feminist literary criticism, reception studies, archival practices, translation, publishing histories, readerships, pedagogy, and comparative perspectives on women's writing across languages and cultures.

items.fullDescription: This panel at the SAMLA 97 conference (Nov. 5-7, 2026) examines hospitality as a critical framework within postcolonial studies, attending to the ways literary and cultural texts negotiate welcome, refusal, obligation, and exposure in the aftermath of empire. Within empire studies, hospitality emerges as a fraught ethical and aesthetic relation shaped by colonial histories, uneven mobility, and asymmetrical power. We consider how such texts register these dynamics across postcolonial contexts. Readings across genres and media—including novels, graphic novels, life writing, film, poetry, and theory—deploy hospitality as a vital lens for understanding postcolonial subjectivity, relationality, and the ongoing legacies of colonial governance.

items.fullDescription: 2026 marks multiple milestones for the legacy of legendary Afrofuturist author Octavia E. Butler: fifty years since the publication of her first novel, Patternmaster (1976), and the twentieth anniversary of her passing, on February 24, 2006. It also marks the announcement of a film version of one of her most famous and most prophetic works, Parable of the Sower (1993), as well as the surprise republishing of her disavowed novel, Survivor (1978), for the first time since the 1980s. These anniversaries arrive at a moment when Butler’s work continues to gain public attention for its prescience and political relevance, and as she continues to amass scholarly attention across scholarly disciplines.

Essays should cover multiple genres (film, fiction, television, graphic novels, music) and a broad time period. These are overviews or research starters rather than narrow analyses.

items.fullDescription: Virginia Woolf’s Mrs Dalloway (1925) remains a cornerstone of the modernist literary canon. Often celebrated as Woolf’s greatest novel, the plot is set in post-World War I London and revolves around a single day in the life of its protagonist, Mrs Clarissa Dalloway, as she prepares to host an evening party. Beneath its seemingly simple plot, the novel exemplifies a profound exploration of consciousness, time, and the inner lives of characters through its extensive use of the stream-of-consciousness technique. Although narrated in a third-person omniscient voice, the narrative primarily focuses on the inner consciousness of the characters.

items.excerpt: Pendant la période de l'Indochine française, les premiers vietnamiens sont acheminés en Nouvelle-Calédonie en tant que travailleurs sous-contrat dans les mines de nickel. Cette période marque en parallèle la rébellion très présente des autochtones de l'île - les Kanak - face à la dépossession territoriale des colons français. Le...
items.fullDescription: Pendant la période de l'Indochine française, les premiers vietnamiens sont acheminés en Nouvelle-Calédonie en tant que travailleurs sous-contrat dans les mines de nickel. Cette période marque en parallèle la rébellion très présente des autochtones de l'île - les Kanak - face à la dépossession territoriale des colons français. Le refus de participer à l'industrie minière d'un groupe favorise l'expatriation de l'autre sur l'archipel en pleine ère de la colonisation française dans plusieurs colonies. Le roi tonkinois Ham Nghi, à l'âge de 18 ans, a été placé en exil en Algérie après des années de mouvements de résistance contre la puissance coloniale française. Son expulsion fait écho à celle de la Reine malgache Ranavalona III, qui a eu lieu après l'annexion de Madagascar par la France en 1896. L'ensemble de l'empire français, étendu de l'Indochine à l'Afrique insulaire, est sujet à la déportation dans la plus grande colonie de peuplement. Ce réseau de déportation des colonies liées met en avant la question des outils de domination employés par le système colonial. Malgré les différentes populations colonisées, la stratégie de domination s'applique d'une certaine façon. **** Dans le cadre académique au sein des études francophones et françaises, nous voyons souvent des pistes de spécialisation distinctes, telles que les études européennes, les études caribéennes, et les études postcoloniales francophones. Les cours d'histoire et de littérature offrent également des réflexions particulières selon une région spécifique ainsi qu'une époque donnée. L'objectif de former les futurs chercheurs dans des domaines précis, faute d'études approfondies sur les caractéristiques transcontinentales, risque de faire oublier qu'au coeur de l'histoire de l'un est tissée celle de l'autre. Cette imbrication transnationale et transcommunautaire, inévitable soit-elle, est due à la fois aux déplacements migratoires, à l'exil d'après-guerre, et à la mobilisation de la force de travail par le système capitaliste et colonial. Lorsque l'on met en évidence ce lien intrinsèque entre des événements, on fait émerger un réseau transcolonial qui lie l'Afrique, l'Asie, et/ou l'Europe. Ce réseau nous oblige à réfléchir à l'interconnexion de l'un à l'autre, d'une communauté spécifique à une autre soi-disant éloignée. Dans le contexte du monde d'aujourd'hui, une telle conscience de l'interconnexion des histoires coloniales peut susciter des réflexions : Peut-on vraiment ignorer le combat de l'autre ? Quels seront les enjeux ? Communications à envoyer soit en anglais, soit en français.

items.title: Migration from Kurdistan and Brain Drain: Factors, Challenges and Prospects
items.location: Univeristy of Kurdistan, KRI, Iraq · Univeristy of Kurdistan
items.excerpt: Call for Papers Migration from Kurdistan and Brain Drain: Factors, Challenges and Prospects (December 1st, 2026) The University of Kurdistan Hewlêr (UKH) invites scholars, researchers, policymakers, practitioners, and members of the Kurdish diaspora to submit abstracts for an international interdisciplinary conference on Migration from Kurdistan and...
items.fullDescription: Call for Papers Migration from Kurdistan and Brain Drain: Factors, Challenges and Prospects (December 1st, 2026) The University of Kurdistan Hewlêr (UKH) invites scholars, researchers, policymakers, practitioners, and members of the Kurdish diaspora to submit abstracts for an international interdisciplinary conference on Migration from Kurdistan and Brain Drain: Factors, Challenges and Prospects. We welcome theoretically informed and empirically grounded papers that examine how brain drain, and brain waste are shaped by conflict and displacement, labor-market pressures, governance and legal frameworks, statelessness, social networks, gendered and generational inequalities, and the health and psychosocial consequences of mobility. Contributions may address nationalism, diaspora identity, intersectionality, return and re-return, remittances and knowledge migration, and the institutional conditions that enable retention, legal protection, and reintegration. The conference aims to advance comparative and policy-relevant debate on migration not only as a question of departure, but also of rights, belonging, development, and future-making in Kurdish society and beyond. This conference aims to bring together scholars, practitioners, and researchers to examine Kurdish migration in its full complexity: its drivers and consequences, its gendered and generational dimensions, and the conditions under which departure can give way to circulation, return, and knowledge transfer. We invite empirically rigorous and theoretically informed contributions from across disciplines, including but not limited to sociology, economics, law, public health, political science, and psychology. Submissions are welcome in English only. We invite potential contributors to submit papers on the following (but not limited to) topics: Intersectionality and migration Skilled Migration: Brain Drain vs. Brain Gain Brain-drain from KRI: factors and incentives Return migration to KRI: challenges and opportunities Legal and political challenges Socio-economic challenges Nationalism, patriotism and the Kurdish diaspora Kurdish diaspora identity and transnational belonging Divisions within Kurdish diaspora: political and cultural disparity Language and cultural loss among second-generation Kurds Psychological context of migration Health and mental health of refugees Higher education Comparative perspectives from other regions and contexts University of Kurdistan Hewlêr welcomes innovative and interdisciplinary approaches to research and encourages submissions that engage with the conference theme from diverse perspectives, including topics not explicitly listed above. Conference Format The conference is planned to take place in Erbil, Kurdistan Region of Iraq, in December 1st, 2026. The deadline for abstract submission is on 01/09/2026 at 12:00 PM (Erbil time, GMT+3) The deadline for full paper submissions is on 01/11/2026 at 12:00 PM (Erbil time, GMT+3) AI Usage Policy To adhere to the best academic practices in research integrity, all submissions must adhere to the following standards concerning AI usage. AI usage, as determined by Turnitin AI detection services, must not exceed 20%, in part or whole of the submitted document. UKH measures large research outputs in either 10- or 20-page increments (splits) when applying this policy. UKH does not recognize any distinction between AI generation and AI editing. Citation and Reference Policy References and citations must be formatted according to the Harvard referencing style. All citations, either direct quotations or paraphrases, must have the exact page number or range of page numbers from which they came, unless stated in an address specifying the scope or coverage of the source's usage. Linked or databased academic articles or online books which have established page numbers and have been published must be cited and referenced independently as a stand-alone document. Submission Guidelines Abstracts should be between 250-350 words and include an abstract title and 3-5 keywords. A brief author biography (200-250 words) must be submitted in a separate file. Please include your full name, affiliation, and contact details (email address) in your biography file. Format Requirements Submit as two separate PDF files: one for the abstract and one for the biography Abstracts must include an abstract title Use Times New Roman, 12pt font, and 1.5-line spacing File names should follow the format: [LastName]_Abstract.pdf and [LastName]_Bio.pdf Abstracts and author biographies should be submitted as separate PDF files via email to migration.conference@ukh.edu.krd For questions, please contact migration.conference@ukh.edu.krd

items.fullDescription: In her foundational text Immigrant Acts , cultural theorist Lisa Lowe argues that the Asian immigrant exists as a “perpetual foreigner within the body politic,” a figure through whom the nation-state defines its own boundaries and citizenship. For Lowe, Asian American cultural production emerges precisely from this “distance from the national culture,” serving as a site that critiques the state’s false promises of universal inclusion.

items.fullDescription: This panel invites exploration and interrogation of in(hospitable) spaces (political, affective, spatial) and its relationship to identity and trauma. Since writing is a way of exploring, representing, and healing from trauma, papers exploring how writing functions as a form of hospitality are welcome also. I envision exclusion of certain bodies because of racism, sexism, xenophobia, etc as treating said bodies as intruders. Hence, writing one's silenced, displaced self becomes an act of claiming space and rendering inhospitable spaces hospitable. Zimbabwean writer and activist Tsitsi Dangarembga in Black and Female: Essays (2023) defines empire as trauma that has created ruptures for the people who have experienced colonialism's violence and aftermath. She also attributes writing as a way of healing and belonging for the collective whose identities have been negated by the traumas of racism. This panel invites exploration and interrogation of in(hospitable) spaces (political, affective, spatial) and its relationship to identity and trauma. Since writing is a way of exploring, representing, and healing from trauma, papers exploring how writing functions as a form of hospitality are welcome also. I envision exclusion of certain bodies because of racism, sexism, xenophobia, etc as treating said bodies as intruders. Hence, writing one's silenced, displaced self becomes an act of claiming space and rendering inhospitable spaces hospitable. Possible topics include: · Black women's writing and healing from trauma · Postcolonial ruptures including war and political repression · Refugees, migrants, camps · Racism, alienation, and trauma

items.fullDescription: Setting the stage: a theatre and performance dissertation writing group Writing a dissertation can be lonely: we want to change that. Many of us first fell in love with theater because — through exploratory rehearsals, late-night tech runs, and joyful opening nights — we found camaraderie and connection, both of which can feel distant in graduate school. If theater is best practiced with others, how can our research and writing processes be shaped by the same commitment to community-building?

items.fullDescription: Marianne Moore 2027, hosted by the Faculty of English at the University of Cambridge, will follow the highly successful formats of previous Moore conferences (Penn State 2003, Houston 2015, Buffalo 2020, Stanford 2025) with a schedule of panels, round tables and Q&As for a single audience over two and a half days. Exactly 100 years after Moore herself visited Cambridge, the purpose of the conference is to bring together an international and various group of readers with a strong predilection for thinking and writing about poetry, and put them to work newly and again on this singular, difficult, and discriminating poet. The schedule will take shape around readings between Marianne Moore and other writers.

items.fullDescription: Abstracts are invited for a traditional panel session to be held at the annual meeting of the South Atlantic Modern Language Association, scheduled for 5-7 November 2026 at the Wyndham Atlanta Buckhead Hotel and Conference Center, Atlanta, GA, USA. This session intends to explore the theme of “hospitality” in the works of Joseph Conrad in order to highlight how Conrad’s relationships both reflected and influenced his literary output throughout his career. Some relationships were more enduring than others, but all had an impact, often a profound impact, on his life and writing.

items.fullDescription: Rome and Capri, 21–28 June, 2027 F. Scott Fitzgerald visited Italy on two distinct occasions: first in the spring of 1921 during his and Zelda’s long-deferred honeymoon trip, and again from late 1924 to early 1925 during his unsuccessful attempt to economize. While the Fitzgeralds made brief stops in Venice, San Remo, Portofino, Savona, Pisa, Fiesole, Florence, and Naples, it was in the two host sites of this conference—Rome and Capri—that he spent the most time. It was here that he found inspiration for some of his most celebrated works, as well as several others that remain underexplored.

items.fullDescription: We invite you to submit an abstract and bio for the panel “ Yesteryear: Cultural and Literary Possibilities”. We are seeking papers that address Caro Claire Burke’s breakout debut novel Yesteryear and its implications on literature and current American culture. How does Burke address established literary conventions? How might one conceive of the evocative narrative style of this novel? How does Burke conceptualize the conscription of motherhood, suppressed queerness, and conservative Christianity? How might the work fit into literary genealogies of sanity, purity, racial prejudice, and womanhood? Proposals should include a title, abstract, your name, and affiliation.

items.fullDescription: The Department of English-Language Literatures and Cultures and the Institute of Literary Studies at UWM are pleased to invite you to an international interdisciplinary conference on H.G. Wells and Modernity: Texts, Contexts, (Re)visions 22nd-24th Oct. 2026 University of Warmia and Mazury in Olsztyn, Poland Celebrating H.G. Wells's 160th/80th anniversaries of birth and death (1866-1946), this conference invites scholars from across literary studies, linguistics, film studies, political theory, sociology, history, philosophy, and other disciplines to examine modernity's (con)texts through Wells, his contemporaries, and his followers. We aim to consider how their visions reshape understandings of the evolution and devolution of the human, humanity, and society. In many of his literary and non-literary texts, Wells diagnosed the shortcomings of class structures and anticipated modernity's ills, imaginatively prefiguring many current ways of conceptualising the human in relation to the nonhuman. He started as one of the most prolific and versatile short story writers of the late nineteenth century, and he ended as one of the most celebrated sociological and political writers of the first half of the twentieth century. His views, shaped by a complex and troubled faith in science and reason, helped crystallize the formulation of pivotal documents such as the Universal Declaration of Human Rights. He died disillusioned that the new world which he had envisioned had not arrived, or worse, frustrated to see his darkest predictions materialise. Undermined and still overlooked as a literary figure by the modernists, who could not see eye to eye with him despite initial friendships, who is H.G. Wells for posterity, that is, our times? In asking this question, we recognize that Wells's legacy cannot be confined to Anglophone literary history alone, and it still speaks urgently to contemporary crises of technology, social fragmentation and political imagination. We especially welcome approaches that could help us position Wells and other modernity/modernist writers within transnational literary networks and explore their influence on and dialogue with world literatures. These topics and others are most welcome, among the proposed conference focus points related to H.G. Wells: ? Author's sources: from antiquity to Jonathan Swift, Charles Dickens, Edgar Allan Poe, and others. ? Modernity and the new ways of writing: short story, scientific romance, science fiction. ? Modern aesthetics and the fictional world: literary and linguistic patterns. Representations of the human and humanity: ecocritical, cognitive, postcolonial, and other postmodern and post-postmodern re-readings. ? Wells and international fantastic and science fiction: comparative perspectives across French (Jules Verne), German, and other contexts. ? Wells's legacy in Polish literature: Stanis?aw Lem and others. ? Dialogues and contentions with Joseph Conrad, E.M. Forster, Virginia Woolf, and Henry James. ? Literary and linguistic depictions of science, catastrophe, and war. ? Shifting cultural concepts: evolution and devolution, tensions of power, ambiguities of colonial appropriation. ? Intermedia reinterpretations: film, graphic novels, games, and other media. ? Wells in translation. ? Political thought and the concept of world state. ? Models of femininity and social change. ? Wells and his engagement with history and the future. ? Utopias and dystopias of Wells and other writers. Proposals for a twenty-minute paper (about 200 words) and a bio note (100 words) should be sent to hgwellsconference@uwm.edu.pl by 10th September 2026. Notification of acceptance by 14th September 2026. Conference fee (non-refundable) payable by 20th September 2026. The account number will be provided upon acceptance. items.fullDescription: Congreso Estética(s) del Horror: Filosofía, Cine y Literatura. Invitación a envío de propuestas. Congreso presencial (English version below) En los últimos años, el terror ha experimentado una renovada visibilidad cultural. El éxito del cine reciente, la consolidación de una auténtica edad de oro de las series televisivas y la aparición de nuevas voces literarias, impulsadas también por el trabajo de editoriales especializadas, evidencian que el horror ocupa hoy un lugar central en el imaginario colectivo y mantiene una extraordinaria capacidad para dialogar con las preocupaciones estéticas, sociales y políticas de cada época. Sin embargo, esta vitalidad cultural no siempre ha encontrado un correlato equivalente en el ámbito académico. Aunque en las últimas décadas los estudios sobre el terror han crecido de forma significativa, todavía persiste un amplio espacio para profundizar en sus implicaciones filosóficas y en los múltiples cruces entre el pensamiento, la literatura y el cine. Con el propósito de contribuir a este campo de investigación, el congreso Estética(s) del horror: filosofía, cine y literatura busca reunir a especialistas interesados en explorar las múltiples relaciones entre el terror y la reflexión filosófica, así como sus manifestaciones en la literatura y el cine. Nos interesan tanto los trabajos que analicen la filosofía del terror -sus categorías estéticas, éticas, metafísicas o políticas- como aquellos que descubran el terror dentro de la propia tradición filosófica, examinando la presencia de lo monstruoso, lo ominoso, el trauma o el horror en autores, problemas o corrientes del pensamiento, desde la Antigüedad hasta la filosofía contemporánea. El congreso aspira, así, a constituirse en un espacio interdisciplinario de diálogo en el que confluyan perspectivas filosóficas, literarias y cinematográficas para pensar el terror no como un género marginal, sino como una herramienta privilegiada para comprender las inquietudes fundamentales de nuestro presente y de nuestra tradición intelectual. El congreso Estética(s) del terror: Filosofía, cine y literatura dará la bienvenida a propuestas relacionadas con alguno de los siguientes ejes temáticos: Filosofía del horror/Estética del horror Horror e intermedialidad Horror y sociedad El congreso está organizado por el grupo de investigación ESCON (Estética contemporánea: arte, política y sociedad) del Departamento de Filosofía y Sociedad de la Universidad Complutense de Madrid (España), por el proyecto UCM 2026 "Historia Conceptual del Tiempo. Crítica y Política" (PR10/26-32656), y por el Grupo Grite (Grupo de Investigacion en Terror) de la Universidad de Buenos Aires (Argentina), tendrá lugar los días 3 y 4 de diciembre de 2026 en la Facultad de Filosofia (edificio A) de la Universidad Complutense de Madrid. Las propuestas de comunicación deberán enviarse a [congresoesteticasdelhorror@gmail.com] hasta el 11 de septiembre de 2026, indicando en el asunto del mensaje: "Estéticas del horror". Cada envío deberá consistir en un único archivo en formato PDF que incluya: Un resumen de la propuesta de hasta 300 palabras. Una breve nota biográfica del autor/a (máximo 150 palabras), con indicación de su afiliación institucional, en su caso. Se aceptarán propuestas en castellano o en inglés. Las comunicaciones tendrán una duración aproximada de 20 minutos. La notificación de aceptación será comunicada a los participantes antes del 30 de septiembre de 2026. Rafael Carrión Arias es Doctor en Filosofía y Doctor en Teoría de la Literatura y Literatura Comparada. Es profesor en el Dpto. de Filosofía y Sociedad en la Universidad Complutense de Madrid (UCM) y miembro del Seminario Nietzsche Complutense. Es autor multiples artículos de investigacion, así como de dos monografías: "Historia de la literatura griega: los orígenes del método genealógico en F. Nietzsche" (Peter Lang, Nueva York, 2020) y "Batman and the Shadows of Modernity: A Critical Genealogy of Contemporary Hero in the Age of Nihilism" (Routledge/Taylor&Francis, London, 2025). Fernando Gabriel Pagnoni Berns es Doctor en Artes y doctorando en Historia. Es profesor en la Facultad de Filosofía y Letras de la Universidad de Buenos Aires (Argentina), donde dicta cursos sobre cine de terror internacional. Dirige GRITE (Grupo de Investigacion sobre el terror) y el proyecto "Civilizacion y barbarie en el cine de terror argentino" y es autor de una monografía sobre la serie española de televisión Historias para no dormir (Universidad de Cádiz, 2020). Ha editado, además, volúmenes dedicados al bicentenario a los cineastas James Wan, Wes Craven y Dario Argento, al giallo italiano (University Press of Mississippi), al cómic de terror (Routledge) y sobre el terror báltico. Asimismo, dirige la colección Terror: Estudios Críticos (Universidad de Cádiz), la primera colección académica en España dedicada exclusivamente a los estudios sobre el género de terror. Call for Papers Aesthetics of Horror: Philosophy, Film, and Literature In-Person Conference In recent years, horror has experienced a remarkable cultural resurgence. The success of contemporary horror cinema, the consolidation of what many have described as a new golden age of television series, and the emergence of new literary voices-supported in part by the work of specialized publishers-all attest to horror's central place in the contemporary cultural imagination and to its enduring capacity to engage with the aesthetic, social, and political concerns of each historical moment. Yet this cultural vitality has not always been matched by an equivalent level of scholarly attention. Although horror studies have expanded considerably over the past decades, significant opportunities remain for further exploring the philosophical dimensions of horror and the many intersections between philosophy, literature, and film. With the aim of contributing to this growing field of research, the conference Aesthetics of Horror: Philosophy, Film, and Literature seeks to bring together scholars interested in examining the manifold relationships between horror and philosophical inquiry, as well as its manifestations across literature and cinema. We welcome contributions that address the philosophy of horror-its aesthetic, ethical, metaphysical, or political dimensions-as well as papers that identify horror within the philosophical tradition itself, examining the presence of the monstrous, the uncanny, trauma, or horror in philosophers, philosophical problems, or schools of thought, from Antiquity to contemporary philosophy. The conference aims to provide an interdisciplinary forum for dialogue in which philosophical, literary, and film studies perspectives converge to explore horror not as a marginal genre, but as a privileged lens through which to understand some of the most pressing concerns of both our contemporary world and our intellectual tradition. Topics The conference welcomes paper proposals addressing (but not limited to) the following themes: Philosophy of Horror / Aesthetics of Horror Horror and Intermediality Horror and Society The conference is jointly organized by ESCON (Contemporary Aesthetics: Art, Politics and Society), a research group within the Department of Philosophy and Society at the Complutense University of Madrid (Spain); the UCM 2026 research project Conceptual History of Time: Critique and Politics (PR10/26-32656); and GRITE (Research Group on Horror) at the University of Buenos Aires (Argentina). The conference will take place on 3-4 December 2026 at the Faculty of Philosophy (Building A), Complutense University of Madrid. Submission Guidelines Paper proposals should be submitted to congresoesteticasdelhorror@gmail.com no later than 11 September 2026, with "Aesthetics of Horror" as the subject line. Submissions should consist of a single PDF file containing: An abstract of no more than 300 words. A brief biographical note (maximum 150 words), including the author's institutional affiliation, where applicable. Proposals may be submitted in English or Spanish. Accepted papers will be allocated 20 minutes for presentation. Applicants will be notified of the outcome of the review process by 30 September 2026. Organizers Rafael Carrión Arias Rafael Carrión Arias holds PhDs in Philosophy and in Literary Theory and Comparative Literature. He is a Lecturer in the Department of Philosophy and Society at the Complutense University of Madrid (UCM) and a member of the Complutense Nietzsche Seminar. He is the author of numerous scholarly articles and two monographs: History of Greek Literature: The Origins of the Genealogical Method in F. Nietzsche (Peter Lang, New York, 2020) and Batman and the Shadows of Modernity: A Critical Genealogy of the Contemporary Hero in the Age of Nihilism (Routledge, 2025). Fernando Gabriel Pagnoni Berns Fernando Gabriel Pagnoni Berns holds a PhD in Arts and is currently completing a PhD in History. He teaches at the Faculty of Philosophy and Letters of the University of Buenos Aires (Argentina), where he offers courses on international horror cinema. He is the director of GRITE (Research Group on Horror) and of the research project Civilisation and Barbarism in Argentine Horror Cinema. He is the author of a monograph on the Spanish television series Historias para no dormir (University of Cádiz, 2020). He has also edited volumes devoted to the bicentenary of Frankenstein; to the filmmakers James Wan, Wes Craven, and Dario Argento; to the Italian giallo (University Press of Mississippi); to horror comics (Routledge); and to Baltic horror. In addition, he serves as the general editor of Horror: Critical Studies (University of Cádiz), the first academic book series in Spain devoted exclusively to horror studies.

items.fullDescription: This session extends scholarly conversations on how violence operates in the corpus of poetic and prose Brut texts. We welcome papers exploring violence as a mechanism for securing and projecting political power, as both a destructive force and a tool for empowerment. We also welcome investigations of poetic and rhetorical violence—including on how the translation and redaction of historical texts function as symbolic conquests—and intersections of violence and gender. Suggested topics include: the aesthetics and rhetoric of warfare, conquest, and displacement; gendered dynamics of violence, captivity, and resistance; and the role of environmental devastation (fire, plague) in historiography.

items.fullDescription: This virtual seminar takes its inspiration from Saidiya Hartman’s citation of the Greek etymology of the word chorus : “to dance within an enclosure.” For Hartman, the chorus also furnishes a paradigm for thinking—to play on Jean-Luc Nancy’s notion—Black (non)being singular plural. In Wayward Lives , Hartman suggests that any of the minor figures can stand in for the others, not as a claim of homogeneity, but rather as a contention that their singularity can be read as paradigmatic of waywardness—in its queer heterogeneity—in general. The chorus of black femme and queer youth is thus at once a singular plurality and a plural singularity as they make beautifully riotous lives within the enclosure of social death.

items.excerpt: Historically, researching and teaching the different art forms took place in a well-sealed vacuum: for example, using literary theory to decipher one of the great texts, visual and compositional analysis to examine a sublime painting, or montage splicing to perceive the nuances of film. items.fullDescription: Historically, researching and teaching the different art forms took place in a well-sealed vacuum: for example, using literary theory to decipher one of the great texts, visual and compositional analysis to examine a sublime painting, or montage splicing to perceive the nuances of film. However, it is becoming more difficult to recognize the borders that separate cinema, literature, and the visual arts. Factors including the rise of globalized societies and the emergence of new technology have forced us to reconsider medium specificity and our relationship with creative expressions and works of art.

items.fullDescription: Inclusive teaching and digital accessibility are two important concepts that align with Universal Design for Learning (UDL) principles; they move away from "retrofitting" (i.e., fixing a problem after a student struggles) toward a "proactive” design (e.g., building a system that works for everyone from the start). Inclusive teaching is a pedagogical framework that focuses on designing a learning environment where every student (regardless of race, gender, socioeconomic status, or learning style) feels valued and has an equal opportunity to succeed. Whereas digital accessibility is a technical standard that ensures that digital tools, documents, and platforms are usable by everyone, regardless of ability.

items.fullDescription: This panel session seeks to explore the cultural fascination surrounding "The Backrooms," an internet 'creepypasta' about an abandoned office building with endless corridors that became a popular YouTube series and most recently, a blockbuster horror film. What are the Backrooms? Do they represent an artificial or alien intelligence attempting to simulate reality? Are they a glitch in the imperfect fabric of an unstable universe? Are they a metaphor for the subconscious, techno-paranoia, wasteland necrocapitalism, corporate control, or the internet itself? Or is this all just a retelling of the Greek myth of the Labyrinth for a Gen Z audience?

items.fullDescription: This roundtable discusses the convergence of literary expression and digital activism as means of empowering a community. The shift of passive representation to active agency in the 21st century can be common in the digital space where the local knowledge is stored and disseminated. We discuss how marginalized groups, namely Dalit writers and activists, have used digital humanities to evade authoritarian gatekeepers and develop transnational solidarities. This session welcomes people to talk about how online spaces can be seen as a form of regenerative space where the testimonios of Dalit can be heard, transforming the world into a supportive space of collective action and literary insurgency.

items.fullDescription: On January 19, 2022, Sheila Heti published the first of ten weekly installments of “A Diary in Alphabetical Order” in The New York Times. Each installment included an author’s note explaining that Heti had “loaded all 500,000 words of [her] journals into Excel to order the sentences alphabetically,” hoping to “see the self in a new way.” Over time, the project “grew into something more novelistic,” and the note concludes that “the self’s report on itself is surely a great fiction.” Heti is hardly the first author to challenge or destabilize autobiographical conventions.

items.fullDescription: Drawing inspiration from Adrienne Rich's seminal book Of Woman Born (1976), in 2006, Andrea O'Reilly coined the term "Motherhood Studies" and established a new interdisciplinary academic field aimed at studying "motherhood" as an institution and "mothering" as an experience. O'Reilly emphasized the importance of adopting a feminist framework designed expressly for mothers: a "matricentric feminism" necessary for an intersectional exploration of motherhood. Within "Motherhood Studies," being or not being a mother matters, and the experience of motherhood is treated as a category alongside gender, class, and race.

items.title: When Lives Do Things: Lives as Archives
items.excerpt: As Sidonie Smith and Julia Watson argue, “autobiographical storytelling is a performative act through which narrators construct rather than simply describe selves.” In recent times, “life writing” has become a familiar term in literary and cultural studies, encompassing a range of written texts, including autobiographies, biographies, letters, memoirs, sl...
items.fullDescription: As Sidonie Smith and Julia Watson argue, “autobiographical storytelling is a performative act through which narrators construct rather than simply describe selves.” In recent times, “life writing” has become a familiar term in literary and cultural studies, encompassing a range of written texts, including autobiographies, biographies, letters, memoirs, slave narratives, prison narratives, captivity narratives, travel narratives, confessionals, conversion narratives and even in recent times, personal diaries. However, rather than remain transparent records of lived experience, life writings actively shape identities, produce historical knowledge, preserve cultural memory, and intervene in social and political worlds.

items.fullDescription: The guiding thread of this panel develops around a curious remark by Jacques Rancière that would seem to amend a classically Aristotelian thesis: “man is a political animal because he is a literary animal,” the fundamental condition of which is the “excess of words” constitutive of the human animal’s politicalness. Literature (or poetry) has long been placed in close proximity to politics, as we find in Plato’s Republic , where philosophy and literature—and, more generally, philosophy and politics—turn out to be perennial adversaries.

items.fullDescription: Panel Proposal for the NeMLA Convention, Newport, RI, March 6–9, 2027 Planetary crises—climate change, toxic pollution, biodiversity loss, and energy transition—are profoundly uneven in their distribution. Their material consequences are shaped by infrastructures of class, race, colonial histories, state power, and global capitalism. Increasingly, literary, artistic, and cultural texts across the world foreground these asymmetries, framing ecological crisis not only as environmental catastrophe but as a question of justice: Who breathes polluted air? Who profits from green transformation? Who consumes melting ice? Who bears the social and environmental costs of planetary change?

items.fullDescription: In the definition given by Podnieks, matrifocal literatures are written and narrated by mothers in the first-person or limited third-person voice, rendering maternal identity and experience from subjective perspectives. Such narratives (re)value the significance and meaning of maternal figures in cultural, social, and national arenas, and often contest or negotiate traditional ideologies of the “good” mother as self-sacrificing, nurturing, and sexless with her antithesis, the “bad” mother. In the last few years, we have seen the rise of matrifocal writings published to break taboos about previously silenced reproductive experiences, create a sense of community, support, and care, and, overall, empower people.

Such work is often relegated to women’s labor, caregivers, and hired help who go beyond roles and job duties to make personal connections.

items.fullDescription: Philip Kan Gotanda’s play Yankee Dawg You Die centers a conflict between two Asian American actors. The aging Vincent Chang built his career playing bumbling Japanese soldiers and one-dimensional Asian stereotypes in classic Hollywood. He’s confronted by the up-and-coming Bradley Yamashita, who insists that Asian (American) characters should be played with dignity and accuracy. The heart of the play is an ambivalence about Asian (American) representation and whether viewers should sympathize with Chang for taking the only acting jobs available or with Yamashita in his condemnation of the racist roles—or with both.

She continues by explaining her personal experience with this spatiality, noting that, “Like many of my contemporaries I have yearned to find my place in this world, to have a sense of homecoming, a sense of being wedded to a place” (2). To that end, a commonality shared between all humans is a searching for a place in which we belong, and this is particularly evident in literature that is produced during times of major societal events.

This panel looks to open a discussion about how these and other contemporary novels both explore and challenge the themes of the source material. What do they say about society, race, gender, feminism, and marginalization?

items.fullDescription: In both her life and her literary work, American writer Edith Wharton was a keen observer and recorder of the interrelationship(s) between power and community at the end of the 19th century and throughout the early decades of the 20th. Born into the world of wealth and privilege of “Old New York” society, Wharton grew weary of its longstanding communal norms and constraints. At the same time, however, she was also openly critical of the ostentation and conspicuous consumption on display during the annual Newport, RI “summer season.”

items.fullDescription: In her resolute commitment to telling stories about Black life without the white gaze, Toni Morrison articulated a model of community empowerment grounded in local knowledge, intra-communal collaboration, and the self-reflexive narration of Black worlds. While Morrison remains one of the most visible public advocates of this aesthetic and ethical orientation, practices of Black worldmaking across African and diasporic literatures have long exceeded mere response to anti-Blackness. Even under conditions structured by racial violence, coloniality, displacement, and social rupture, Black communities have continually generated modes of life, relation, memory, and imagination that assert the possibility of worlds otherwise.

items.fullDescription: Beecroft argues in The Cambridge Companion to the Novel that, if we understand the novel as storytelling in any narrative form indigenous to a particular culture or people, then we must acknowledge the existence of multiple novels, each with distinct temporal and spatial origins. He maintains that storytelling traditions are embedded in every society, and that many of these traditions have complemented, intersected with, or diverged from Western narrative forms (46–48). However, the narrative form canonized as the “novel” in the Western literary tradition has a specific historical and aesthetic origin in ancient Greece.

items.fullDescription: Colloque international Cinéma libanais histoires, enjeux et patrimoines cinématographiques Caen - 12-13-14 avril 2027 En 1897, Alexandre Promio, opérateur pour les frères Lumières, pose son cinématographe à Beyrouth. Sur la place des canons (Sahet El Bourj), Promio filme ce qui restera comme la plus ancienne trace animée de la ville. Cette « vue lumière » n'est pas seulement le premier jalon technique du cinéma au Liban ; elle est l'acte de naissance du regard cinématographique sur un territoire qui, pendant 130 ans, ne cessera d'être filmé, modelé et reconstruit par les images. A deux ans du centenaire officiel du cinéma national, marqué par Les Aventures d'Elias Mabrouk (Giordano Pidutti, 1929), ce colloque se veut être une étape préparatoire de réflexion. Entre ce premier film de l'italien Giordano Pidutti et l'échéance symbolique de 2029, le cinéma libanais aurait traversé des cycles de création, d'interruption, de destruction et de renaissance. Comment penser cette histoire lorsque ses premières images ont été produites depuis un regard extérieur, dans un contexte où les conditions de production, de diffusion et de réception du cinéma étaient déjà étroitement liées à des enjeux politiques coloniaux ? Se réunir en 2027 permet de poser les fondements d'une réflexion critique sur ce siècle d'images avant les célébrations officielles, dans un moment où la question de la mémoire nationale est plus brûlante que jamais. Organiser ce colloque répond à un besoin de comprendre les bouleversements artistiques, culturels et politiques contemporains au Liban. Comme Promio qui, en 1897, a saisi le mouvement à Beyrouth, nous cherchons aujourd'hui à saisir les mouvements cinématographiques qui, malgré l'instabilité persistante, refusent de s'éteindre. Notre colloque s'articulera autour de quatre axes complémentaires : I) Le cinéma national et international : Filmer Beyrouth « Beyrouth a longtemps joué le rôle d'orientale favorite de l'Occident. Une favorite sans rivale au Proche-Orient. Ville de toutes les cultures, de toutes les affaires, de tous les plaisirs, mais aussi ville de tous les drames, Beyrouth sera la ville de tous les mythes ». C'est avec ces mots que Jocelyne Saab et Roland-Pierre Paringaux présentent la capitale libanaise dans leur projet Beyrouth, mille et une images. Ce projet est né de la volonté de créer une Cinémathèque à Beyrouth[1] en regroupant les films occidentaux et orientaux qui ont façonné l'image de la ville. Saab et Paringaux ont programmé en 1993 à la Cinémathèque française et à l'Institut du monde arabe une cinquantaine de films tournés à Beyrouth. En suivant leurs pas, ce premier axe propose d'étudier les images de Beyrouth. Il s'agit d'analyser comment la ville, dépourvue d'identité stable et invariable, devient, à travers les regards des cinéastes, un espace hétéroclite façonné par les affects, plutôt qu'un terrain géographique neutre. Du « simulacre » produit par le cinéma international, pour reprendre les mots du chercheur Elie Yazbek[2], à la « subjectivisation » opérée par le cinéma libanais, Beyrouth apparaît comme une ville-fantôme, sans cesse réinventée par l'objectif. Pour ouvrir la réflexion et guider les futur·e·s intervenant·e·s, cet axe propose quelques pistes de réflexion non exhaustives : · L'évolution de l'image de Beyrouth dans le cinéma · Analyse comparative des méthodes de représentation (cinéma occidental / cinéma libanais, fiction / documentaire, cinéma libanais / cinéma au Liban) · Fragmentation urbaine et cartographie d'une ville sans repères : espace labyrinthique II) Histoire du Liban et Histoire du cinéma : enjeux d'écritures et de représentations Cet axe de recherche examine la relation entre la production filmique et le récit historique au Liban à travers deux approches complémentaires : l'écriture cinématographique de l'histoire du pays et l'écriture de l'histoire du cinéma libanais. Dans un contexte où l'enseignement de l'histoire nationale présente des lacunes et s'arrête officiellement à l'indépendance de 1943, le cinéma occupe une fonction de représentation essentielle. Il permet de documenter une histoire contemporaine souvent absente des programmes scolaires, devenant ainsi un support pédagogique et social capable de proposer un récit là où le manuel fait défaut. Cette démarche s'inscrit dans les réflexions actuelles, notamment portées par l'Association libanaise d'histoire (ALH), sur la nécessité de former les jeunes générations à l'analyse critique des sources. En apprenant à distinguer l'information de son interprétation tout en respectant la pluralité des expériences vécues, le cinéma peut contribuer à la construction d'une mémoire collective intégrant les différentes réalités d'un pays où l'histoire varie souvent d'une communauté à l'autre. Parallèlement, cet axe étudie les défis méthodologiques liés à la rédaction d'une histoire du cinéma libanais. La production cinématographique nationale se caractérise par son aspect fragmenté et éparpillé, ce qui rend complexe la constitution d'une chronologie unifiée. Bien que des chercheurs et chercheuses tels que Mohamed Soueid, Ibrahim al-Ariss, Hady Zaccak, Lina Khatib, Elie Yazbek ou Raphaël Millet, aient publié des ouvrages de référence pour structurer cette mémoire, l'éparpillage des films et des sources primaires demeure un obstacle majeur. Il s'agira ici de rassembler ces fragments de patrimoine pour établir une historiographie du cinéma libanais. L'objectif est de comprendre comment ces deux formes d'écritures s'influencent mutuellement pour définir l'identité culturelle et historique du Liban. Pour guider les communications, cet axe propose quelques pistes de réflexion non exhaustives : · La représentation cinématographique de l'histoire du Liban · L'historiographie du cinéma libanais : défis et méthodes · Cinéma libanais et mémoire collective III) Pratiques artistiques contemporaines Les pratiques contemporaines de l'image nous invitent à penser le cinéma libanais actuel dans d'autres espaces que celui de la salle de projection traditionnelle : lieu d'exposition, réseaux sociaux, plateformes en ligne sont autant de zones - réelles ou virtuelles - au sein desquelles le cinéma libanais se transforme. Des installations multimédia de Lamia Joreige aux films-performances de Rabih Mroué et Lina Saneh, l'objet-film dépasse le cadre classique de la narration pour devenir un lieu d'expérimentation et de recherches. Irriguées par des pratiques d'hybridation, de « trafiquage », de fragmentation, les démarches des cinéastes libanais contemporains (tels que Sirine Fattouh, Ghassan Salhab, Akram Zaatari, Joana Hadjithomas et Khalil Joreige, etc) nous invitent à repenser notre rapport aux images, au réel et à la fiction. Les communications pourront proposer tous types d'approches méthodologiques (analyse filmique, approche génétique, recherche-création, études culturelles). Pour orienter les futur•es intervenant•es, nous proposons quelques pistes de réflexion non exhaustives : · Hybridation des formes : l'image comme terrain d'expérimentation · Les représentations filmiques contemporaines face aux représentations médiatiques · Politiques des images, éthiques du regard et nouveaux récits IV) Les pratiques archivistiques autour du cinéma libanais Des documents de travail en amont du tournage à ceux qui accompagnent la diffusion des œuvres, les archives non-film du cinéma libanais constituent des ressources majeures pour la recherche en études cinématographiques. Appliquées au cas libanais, elles offrent la possibilité de retracer la circulation des films tout en éclairant les conditions matérielles, institutionnelles et discursives de leur production et de leur réception à différentes époques. À partir de la critique de presse, des affiches, des photographies de plateau et de promotion, des carnets de notes, des scénarios annotés, de la correspondance professionnelle ou de tout autre document afférent à la fabrique et à la circulation des œuvres, cet axe invite à interroger les archives non-film du cinéma libanais afin d'en dégager les régimes de réception successifs, ainsi que les modalités d'accès, différenciées selon les aires géographiques et les périodes historiques, tant aux films eux-mêmes qu'aux savoirs qui s'élaborent à leur sujet. Parallèlement, la préservation de ces fonds mobilise au Liban un ensemble de structures engagées dans un travail soutenu de collecte, d'indexation, de restauration, de numérisation et de valorisation. L'Association Jocelyne Saab, UMAM Documentation and Research, Nadi lekol el Nas, Cinémathèque Beyrouth, l'Arab Image Foundation (AIF), la Bibliothèque Orientale, les Non-Aligned Film Archives, ainsi que plusieurs collections privées (Abboudi Abou Jaoudé, Hady Zaccak, etc.) constituent autant d'instances à partir desquelles se reformulent aujourd'hui les enjeux de conservation, de restitution et de transmission des archives. Les communications pourront mobiliser une pluralité d'approches méthodologiques (analyse filmique, approche génétique, recherche-création, inventaire et valorisation de corpus au sein des organisations, études culturelles). Afin d'orienter les futur·es intervenant·es, nous proposons quelques pistes de réflexion, à titre non exhaustif : · La restitution des archives non-films et l'enjeu de leur circulation · Pratiques Archivistiques : Collecte, Préservation, conservation, restauration et valorisation des archives · Politiques des archives : le patrimoine libanais, les institutions nationales et les structures culturelles indépendantes Les propositions de communication, d'une longueur de 300 à 500 mots, accompagnées d'une courte biographie de l'auteur (150 mots), sont à envoyer avant le 30 septembre 2026 à colloquecinemalibanais@gmail.com Les communications ne devront pas dépasser les 20 minutes. Une réponse sera communiquée avant le 31 octobre 2026. Les frais de déplacement seront à la charge des participants. Comité d'organisation : Evelyne Hlais Université Saint-Joseph de Beyrouth, Université de Caen - Normandie, Michael Issa El Helou Université Saint-Joseph de Beyrouth, Université de Caen - Normandie, Rosalie Tenaillon Chackal Université Saint-Joseph de Beyrouth, Université de Poitiers, Hady Zaccak Université Saint-Joseph de Beyrouth, Université Paris 1 Panthéon-Sorbonne. items.fullDescription: CFP for NeMLA 2027 Conference Virtual Only Panel Oral Community Knowledge as Antidote for Empire in Anglophone Literature If Empire thrives and even conquers through written text: codification of laws and bureaucracy, cartography of territory, travelogues of the exotic Other, it equally seeks to suppress oral indigenous knowledge of the vanquished. Yet orality can also be the site of resistance and community identity for just such a reason. This panel seeks proposals of works that investigate how orality of form and/or content in Anglophone literature disrupts Empire and empowers community. The irony of pursuing orality in literature, which is historically written, is not lost on us, but also provides an opportunity for creative, resilient exploration. Some possible ways of approaching this topic may include: Accent and dialect Pidgins, creoles, and contact languages Translations and translators Language and identity Oral folk wisdom and stories Glossaries and indices Textual hybridity or multimodality Literacy This is a Call for Papers for the NeMLA 2027 Conference. Submissions will only be accepted through the NeMLA portal, with a deadline of September 30, 2026 . This panel is Virtual Only , regardless of whether the participant is physically present at the conference. Paper proposals can be submitted here: https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22648. Only submissions submitted through the NeMLA portal will be considered. For questions, please feel free to email Dr. Dorisa Costello at dcostello@jessup.edu.

items.fullDescription: Affect theory has grown largely within Euro-American frameworks, from Berlant's neoliberal America to Stewart's account of the American ordinary. Sara Ahmed's work on the cultural politics of emotion has been pivotal in reorienting the field toward race, migration, and colonial power, showing how feelings attach differently to racialized and gendered bodies. This panel builds on that reorientation. It asks what happens when affect theory travels more fully into postcolonial and diasporic contexts, where the body in question has been shaped by colonial histories, forced migration, and asymmetrical encounters with Western institutions. How do structures of feeling shift under these conditions, and where do existing frameworks reach their limit? The panel takes postcolonial and diasporic texts as active interlocutors, capable of pressuring, revising, and extending theory rather than simply illustrating it. We invite papers that attend to the specific conditions under which feeling circulates, accumulates, and fails to discharge in migrant and postcolonial experience. How does testimony feel from the position of the postcolonial subject? What does care cost in communities shaped by colonial injury and displacement? Objects, spaces, and bodies carry affective residue across cultures and histories in ways that ask for new forms of attention and recognition. Papers might engage fiction, film, digital media, and visual culture from postcolonial and diasporic traditions, attending to the affective dimensions of migrant and colonial experience. We welcome contributions grounded in affect theory, postcolonial studies, comparative literature, and media studies. Graduate researchers and faculty are encouraged to apply.

items.fullDescription: This panel, commemorating the centenary of the 1927 publication of "Being and Time," has a general and specific focus. Generally, we will explore the relationship between literature and philosophy without historical and geographical limitations. Specifically, we wish to commemorate the centenary of the publication of Heidegger's "Sein und Zeit"/Being and Time in 1927 as it relates to the practice and theory of literature. Our "specific" avenue of exploration involves at least four possible thematic contexts: · Literary influences on Heidegger. The existential analyses in "Being and Time" reflect many well-known and researched themes such as anxiety, death, dread, fear, curiosity, alienation, the crowd, boredom, responsibility, the experience of time and many others. One might explore the cultural and literary origins of these terms that in "BT" are seen in an ontological perspective. · Literary influences of Heidegger. Many novelists and poets have been influenced by Heidegger. Examples might be Günther Grass, Sartre, Alain Robbe-Grillet, Claude Simon, Iris Murdoch, Walter Percy, Milan Kundera, Jon Fosse, J.M. Coetzee and many others. · Thematic Connections between "Being and Time" and novelists and poets. Virtually all literary modernism and post-modernism explore the theme of temporality and presentations on these connections would be valued. · The influence of "Being and Time" on literary theory after 1927. The literary reflection of Derrida, Deleuze, Foucault, Lacan, Sartre and many others has been influenced by Heidegger's "Being and Time." While the language of this session will be English, we welcome the use of the German text as well as primary texts in languages other than English. These texts will be circulated before the March 2027 conference.

items.fullDescription: Conference: 2-4 December 2026, in Gdansk, Poland and online CALL FOR PAPERS: In our modern world, which some have argued to be disjointed while immersing itself ever deeper in crisis, the turning back to "the olden days" and the ensuing nostalgia constitute anoticeable phenomenon, both individually (the memory of biography) and collectively (the memory of history). Another important - and equally noticeable - phenomenon is the longing for something vague, indefinite or never existent. Hence, during our interdisciplinary conference we would like to concentrate on the phenomena of nostalgia and melancholy. We are interested in all expressions of longing forthe past, from the reassuring and action-propelling ones to those which paralyze us, leading to despair and utter dejection. We aim to explore the experience of nostalgia and melancholy in its multifarious manifestations: psychological, social, historical, cultural, philosophical, religious, economic, political, artistic, and many others. We will also devote considerable attention to how these phenomena appear and transform in artistic practices: literature, film, theatre, and visual arts. This is why we invite researchers representing various academic disciplines: anthropology, history, psychology, psychoanalysis, sociology, politics, philosophy, economics, law, literary studies, theatre studies, film studies, memory studies, nostalgia studies, migration studies, consciousness studies, dream studies, gender studies, postcolonial studies, medical sciences, psychiatry, cognitive sciences, design, project management and other disciplines. Different forms of presentations are encouraged, including case studies, theoretical investigations, problem-oriented arguments, and comparative analyses. Rocks, minerals, fossils, sediments, ruins, and other more-than-living material formations are no longer understood merely as settings or symbols, but as archi...
items.fullDescription: Across contemporary literary and cultural studies, increasing attention has been devoted to the ways in which geological matter shapes cultural imagination, environmental memory, and literary form. Rocks, minerals, fossils, sediments, ruins, and other more-than-living material formations are no longer understood merely as settings or symbols, but as archives of deep time, environmental change, historical violence, and planetary entanglement. This panel invites papers exploring how literature engages with the Earth as a site of memory, relation, and meaning-making. We are particularly interested in approaches that examine geological formations, landscapes, material traces, planetary archives, and sedimented histories as participants in literary and cultural processes rather than passive backdrops to human experience. Possible topics include geologies of memory; deep time and literary temporality; environmental trauma and material archives; fossils, ruins, and traces; sacred landscapes and earth-based imaginaries; extraction, waste, and environmental justice; posthumanist and new materialist approaches to literature; relational poetics; and the intersections of geology, ecology, and cultural memory. We welcome contributions addressing literary and cultural texts from any historical period, linguistic tradition, or geographical region, as well as comparative and transnational perspectives. The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included. Please clearly indicate in your submission whether you wish to participate in person or remotely. All abstract submissions must be submitted through the NeMLA portal https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22630. Please note that the submission window for individual abstracts officially opens June 15 and closes September 30.

items.fullDescription: This panel presents a review of the musical, artistic, literary, social, political, and cultural expressions of Bad Bunny, Benito Antonio Martinez Ocasio. The panel centers on aspects of African diasporic experiences, i.e., Afro-Latinidad. The panel considers Blackness as gleaned and embedded from Bad Bunny's musical repertoire and uses Aurora Santiago Ortiz and Jorell Melendez-Badillo's framework of "historical memory" and "limits of nostalgia" as presented in their piece "(Nada es) 'Como antes.'" The panel seeks to utilize this framework and focus on Black presence in all its intersecting and non-intersecting forms involving race, class, gender, and sex. Specifically, papers are sought that examine strategies and uses of, for example, an "idyllic past" and/or "Great Mixed Family" motif and show ruptures in representations that subsume or otherize Blackness. Submissions can interpret outside of Bad Bunny and include other artists venturing into its use in other realms and/or aspects of appreciation or appropriation discourse, read broadly and sublimated in text. The panel discusses the uses of various musical, visual, aural, and literary works to deconstruct language use, song beats, films, concerts, and media, similar to Bad Bunny's use of House, Hip Hop, Trap, Drill, Reggaeton, Bachata, Afro Punk, etc. A key element is locating, gleaning, or demystifying aspects of Blackness, construed broadly, in Bad Bunny's context or in other works that offer perspectives on Black embeddedness and involve authenticity, appreciation, and the intersection of race, class, gender, or sex. Papers are sought that review the application, infusion, or parallelism of the Black past infusing Bomba, Plena, Son, Salsa, Trap, Drill, Reggaeton, and other genres, as merged running threads. This panel invites papers that explore the musical, artistic, literary, social, political, and cultural expressions of Bad Bunny (Benito Antonio Martinez Ocasio). We focus on African diasporic, or AfroLatinidad, experiences. The panel examines how Blackness construed in various forms appears in Bad Bunny's music and/or is subsumed within it. The panel delves into the intersections of race, class, gender, and sex, and the recognition of the African presence, identity, or representation.

items.fullDescription: In Spring of 2026, the first stage of NASA's Artemis mission captured photographs of the Earth from space, a collective earthling selfie that momentarily transcended the squalor and congestion of the daily news cycle. While satellite imagery, Google maps, and weather forecasting technology have made representations of the global scale relatively commonplace, the astronaut photos have a human presence that allows them to capture something of the sublimity of what it means to live on a planet. The nature of the planetary perspective is epistemologically elusive, being difficult and maybe impossible to reconcile with the phenomenology of lived experience. It is also inherently polysemous, being open to numerous, ramifying, and conflicting interpretations, indicating both mankind's puniness and its awesome technological power, providing a meta-explanation for the reality of everything while being simultaneously abstract and largely irrelevant to everyday life, suggesting a deep connection between human beings and Earth while also overriding Earth toward the project of discovering new and improved planetary homes. Gayatri Chakrovarty Spivak positions inherent alterity at the center of what she calls "planet-thought": "The planet is in the species of alterity, belonging to another system; and yet we inhabit it, on loan. … If we imagine ourselves as planetary subjects … alterity remains underived from us; it is not our dialectical negation, it contains us as much as it pushes us away." Cinema is the art of representing the world through an uncanny simulacrum of itself, which may make it a particularly apt medium for capturing the ambivalent and paradoxical alterity that defines the ontology of the planetary imagination. Indeed, one of the first works of narrative cinema, Georges Méliès's A Trip to the Moon (1902), is structured around visionary depictions of Earth from space that uncannily anticipate the Apollo and Artemis photos while also inspiring a rich filmic legacy of representations of planetarity. The narrative and imagery of such films has helped to shape the popular perception of what it means to live on a planet, and at their best, these films engage the kinds of contradictions, paradoxes, and ambiguities that Spivak and other cultural studies theorists have explored. This panel will focus on what movies about planets can teach us about what planets are and how to live on them. We accept proposals about movies that foreground telluric-scale events on earth (Armageddon [1998], Don't Look Up [2021], etc.), movies about space exploration (Apollo 13 [1995], Gravity [2013], The Martian [2015], etc.), or movies set on alien planets (Star Wars, Dune, etc.). Any other approaches to examining the intersection between cinema and planetarity are also welcome. Please submit a 300-word abstract by September 30, 2026. Contact Dr. Randy Laist with questions: rlaist@bridgeport.edu.

items.fullDescription: In 1996, Carl Sagan warned us of a time when people would lose their ability to question, “when awesome technological powers are in the hands of a very few . . . when, clutching our crystals and nervously consulting our horoscopes, our critical faculties in decline, unable to distinguish between what feels good and what’s true, we slide, almost without noticing, back into superstition and darkness.” Maybe Mr Sagan was somewhat pessimistic when writing The Demon-Haunted World. But then again, in times of conspiracy theories, Ancient Aliens, Antivaxxers, and Holocaust Deniers, the Age of Information appears just as capable of spreading dangerous frauds and charlatans as it is of disseminating reliable scholarship.

items.title: Reframing Relevance: Sustaining the Humanities in Specialized Academic Contexts
items.location: Newport, RI · Northeast Modern Language Association Conference
items.excerpt: As some institutions restructure academic priorities in response to various pressures in higher education, the Humanities can face contraction, marginalization, or elimination. These challenges are intensified at specialized universities, meaning institutions oriented toward fields such as STEM, health sciences, business, etc., where Humanities courses ar...
items.fullDescription: As some institutions restructure academic priorities in response to various pressures in higher education, the Humanities can face contraction, marginalization, or elimination. These challenges are intensified at specialized universities, meaning institutions oriented toward fields such as STEM, health sciences, business, etc., where Humanities courses are often kept to general education requirements and could be perceived as peripheral to students' professional training. Within such environments, Humanities faculty must navigate not only broader disciplinary challenges but also institutional cultures that may misunderstand or underappreciate the field's contributions. This roundtable invites faculty working in specialized institutional contexts to reflect on how they sustain and advocate for the Humanities amid these pressures. How do instructors articulate the intellectual, ethical, and professional value of Humanities study to students primarily focused on career-focused pathways? What pedagogical or curricular strategies have proven effective in demonstrating relevance to disciplines such as engineering, nursing, or business? Alternatively, how have faculty members built collaborations with colleagues across fields to integrate Humanities perspectives into specialized curricula? Participants will also consider how they engage multiple audiences-students, faculty peers, and administrators-to communicate the significance of Humanities education. By sharing concrete strategies, institutional experiences, and models of interdisciplinary partnership, this discussion aims to highlight adaptable approaches for maintaining the visibility and importance of the Humanities in specialized settings. Ultimately, the roundtable seeks to reassert the Humanities as essential to professional education. This roundtable is part of the Northeast Modern Language Association conference, which will take place between March 6-9, 2027 in Newport, RI. Hybrid: The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included. 250-word abstracts for participation in this roundtable are welcome, and they can be submitted through this website: https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/login

items.fullDescription: Call for Proposals NeMLA's 58th Annual Convention - "Empowering Communities" March 6-9, 2027 | Salve Regina University, Newport, Rhode Island Roundtable Title: Writing and Wellbeing: A New Urgency for Human-Centered Composition Pedagogies Primary Area / Secondary Area: Rhetoric & Composition / Pedagogy & Professional Modality: In person only Chair: Miranda Lutyens, University of Massachusetts Amherst College is a time of excitement and novelty, but it can also bring about anxiety and uncertainty. Those unsettling emotions have been on an upward trend in recent years, with a host of factors - the fallout of the pandemic, social media's curation of idealized lives, and existential fears relating to climate change, generative AI, and political turmoil - raising concerns about the wellbeing of students. The college writing classroom has a long history of offering compassion through creative (and even countercultural) approaches to teaching and learning. This included affirming the rights of linguistically marginalized students to their own language, as well as efforts to keep struggling writers in school to keep them out of the Vietnam War. As we collectively face new urgencies, this roundtable session invites participants to share research projects and instructional practices that consider college writing classrooms as affective and embodied spaces. Recognizing concerns such as instructors' emotional labor and under-resourced writing programs, the session primarily focuses on putting forward innovative approaches to making writing instruction responsive to students' experiences in increasingly uncertain times. Insights gained from critical teacher research on human-centered pedagogies are particularly welcome, as are contributions from all levels of writing instruction, including first-year composition, writing in the disciplines, writing support for graduate students etc. The goal of this roundtable is for both participants and attendees to leave with expanded perspectives on how the college writing classroom might offer students more pleasure and new possibilities - to reimagine a more salutary relationship to writing, to engage with the written word in more personally meaningful ways, and to gain a greater awareness of themselves and others. The roundtable also aims to offer practical strategies for enacting - or at least experimenting with - new classroom practices. Submission Deadline: September 30, 2026 Please submit abstracts via the NeMLA portal: https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22547 Convention information can be found here: https://www.nemla.org/convention.html

items.excerpt: Hybrid: The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included. Primary Areas of interest: Global Anglophone / Cultural Studies and Media Studies Abstract: Since at least the middle of the 20th century, we have seen few, if any, utopian texts speculating about positive potential worlds. Instead, we have seen a deluge of dystopia...
items.fullDescription: Hybrid: The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included. Primary Areas of interest: Global Anglophone / Cultural Studies and Media Studies Abstract: Since at least the middle of the 20th century, we have seen few, if any, utopian texts speculating about positive potential worlds. Instead, we have seen a deluge of dystopian, apocalyptic, and post-apocalyptic texts foretelling the end of the world. This begs the question, why have utopian texts become such a scarce literary genre? What is it about our time that makes speculative literature turn away from the utopic and instead toward its more nihilistic counterparts? Our societal fascination with apocalyptic literature has not gone unnoticed. Writing in 1979, H. Bruce Franklin identifies this cultural anxiety in his essay, "What Are We to Make of J. G. Ballard's Apocalypse," where he claims that the post-World War II decolonial liberation movements and the collapse of the European empires were the source of this apocalyptic obsession. On the other hand, the utopian genre is often acknowledged as beginning with Thomas More's Utopia (1516), written within the burgeoning rise of capitalism and the colonial expansion of early 16th century Europe. Hythloday, More's fictional narrator, finds Utopia within the New World, where the undiscovered territories and peoples outside of Europe became the catalysts that made the early utopian imagination possible. That the European utopian genre came at the expense of the existing worlds of indigenous peoples might also prompt us to think about how apocalypse factors into the utopian imagination. Since Franklin's essay, this fixation with the end of the world has only intensified, driven to new heights by the overwhelming evidence of climate change's devastation, global pandemics, and catastrophic geopolitical conflicts. Franklin concludes that Ballard's novels are unable to make a distinction between the end of capitalism and the end of the world itself. This prompts us ask: whose "world" is ending and how might it make way for a new one? Abstracts should be 200-300 words in length, and abstract titles should not exceed 100 characters. All abstracts must be submitted via NeMLA's portal: https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22729
