items.fullDescription: This panel invites papers that examine the agency of marginalized characters in literary, visual, audiovisual, and artistic productions from the French?speaking world across any historical period. Artistic works offer numerous examples of figures who maintain an ambivalent relationship to their communities. Because of their social or ethnic origins, their divergent perspectives, or their transgression of established norms, certain characters find themselves pushed to the margins of social, familial, or professional groups. While such exclusion may appear to weaken those who experience it, this panel asks whether marginalization might also function as a source of strength. Can marginality become a generative position? Do marginalized individuals succeed in forming new communities that enable them to confront stigma and ultimately overcome it? This discussion seeks to shift our understanding of the place occupied by marginalized individuals and groups in fictional narratives. By considering the networks of solidarity that emerge among marginalized subjects, the panel explores what lies beneath their presumed inferiority, inviting critical attention to what is hidden, erased, or rendered invisible within dominant narratives. More importantly, we will examine how marginalized communities may become empowering spaces of resistance, mutual support, and hope. Drawing on approaches from cultural studies, postcolonial theory, sociolinguistics, and intersectionality, this session seeks to understand how literary, visual, audiovisual, and artistic narratives stage the tensions between marginality and belonging, strength and vulnerability. Proposals engaging with environmental humanities, posthumanism, Indigenous studies, as well as diaspora and migration studies are strongly encouraged. We also welcome submissions drawing on gender and queer studies, memory and trauma studies, digital humanities, or other interdisciplinary frameworks within the humanities. We welcome proposals of 250-300 words, in French or English, submitted by September 30.

items.title: Beyond Hegemony: Masculinities in World and Comparative Literature
items.excerpt: This panel examines how literary and cultural texts from diverse global traditions imagine masculinity not merely as an individual identity but as a relational and communal practice. In an era marked by migration, political polarization, social fragmentation, environmental crisis, and renewed struggles for collective empowerment, narratives of masculinity...
items.fullDescription: This panel examines how literary and cultural texts from diverse global traditions imagine masculinity not merely as an individual identity but as a relational and communal practice. In an era marked by migration, political polarization, social fragmentation, environmental crisis, and renewed struggles for collective empowerment, narratives of masculinity have become central sites through which communities negotiate belonging, responsibility, vulnerability, and resistance. While dominant models of masculinity have often been associated with hierarchy, authority, nationalism, and exclusion, contemporary world literatures increasingly depict alternative masculinities grounded in care, ethical responsibility, emotional openness, solidarity, and collective survival. Such representations challenge inherited structures of power while opening new possibilities for community formation and social transformation. This panel invites comparative and transnational approaches that explore how literary texts, films, cultural narratives, and other expressive forms construct, critique, or reimagine masculinities within local and global communities. Particular attention may be given to how gender intersects with race, class, migration, coloniality, religion, ecology, technology, and cultural memory in shaping collective identities and practices of empowerment. Situated within the broader concerns of World Literature and Comparative Literature, this session seeks to foster dialogue across regions, languages, and traditions while investigating how masculinities participate in the creation-or disruption-of empowered communities. We welcome papers addressing, but not limited to: Comparative constructions of masculinity across world literatures Masculinity and migration, diaspora, or displacement Literary representations of fatherhood, mentorship, and communal responsibility Masculinities in Global South literatures Masculinity and environmental or ecological communities Masculinity, memory, and post-conflict reconstruction Queer and non-hegemonic masculinities in world literature Masculinity and cultural resistance under authoritarian regimes Masculinity, religion, and collective belonging Masculinity and affect in contemporary global fiction

items.fullDescription: Our session examines the importance of women as fictional characters, authors, and purveyors of legacies associated with the Great War of 1914-1918. It is hoped that we can bring a fresh critical light to this pivotal moment in world history. Too often, scholarly reflection on the Great War tends to focus military activity and various aspects of masculine performance. We would like to use a wider lens for our discussion of the Great War. Please note the very wide range of perspectives in this seminar: authors, characters, and context. More precisely, our reconsideration of the works, authors, and legacies of the Great War includes three directions: first, the examination of women appearing as fictional characters in this normally male-oriented thematic area; second, reflection on women writers, artists, and filmmakers engaged in representing the Great War; finally, establishing historical continuities deriving from the relationship between women and the Great War. The flexible methodology of this session includes the examination of novels, poems, paintings, photography, film, documentaries, memoirs, and memorials. A few suggestions for interpretative directions: · Recent critical directions on topics relating to women and the Great War · Non-European, global writing on the Great War · Themes of colonialism, migration, class, public health, punition, sexual violence, and race · The inclusion of documentary, archival, and any relevant internet materials · Presentations on "Women, New England, and the Great War" · The involvement of women in medical and public health issues during the Great War · Women in the Great War as represented in museums, local, national, and global media.

items.fullDescription: If you've taught older literature recently, you've probably heard students ask the question "Why am I reading this?" They've got a point. Why should we read Shakespeare's plays, or Paradise Lost , or (god-forbid) "The Rape of the Lock"? We might be able to coax students into reading a Jane Austen novel or even Huckleberry Finn because they are still part of our cultural imagination, but it's less easy to coax students into reading Beowulf or Tom Jones -texts that are less familiar in form and content. As we jettison the canon and the timeless truths of literature, what do we expect reading older texts will give our students that they wouldn't otherwise understand? How do we explain our reasoning to students about why they should read texts from earlier literary periods? How do we present these earlier periods (or periodization) and their culture and ideas as relevant to our undergraduate students in our English, writing, or humanities programs? Or is relevance the problem? How might we redefine older literature in our classes, or what we (can) do with these texts? I'm particularly interested in position papers that take into consideration practical pedagogical and theoretical concerns: arguments about curricula, course development or assignments that do different things with this literature. Papers are welcome on any kind of literature/texts or periods prior to 1800.

items.fullDescription: Collective Selves: Rethinking Autobiography Beyond the Individual. Philippe Lejeune's influential formulation of the autobiographical pact has long shaped autobiography studies. The pact presumes an aligned author, narrator, and protagonist, and simultaneously privileges the singular, first-person life narrative as the normative autobiographical form. However, several personal narratives resist these assumptions. Edited autobiographies, oral histories, collaborative memoirs, and interview-based narratives often construct an autobiographical "I"/protagonist that is multiple rather than singular and that emerges across the voices of individuals whose experiences collectively narrate a shared history. Likewise, some collective life narratives depart from conventional autobiography structures, appearing as fragmented accounts, interwoven testimonies, or dispersed chapters within larger texts. This panel invites papers that examine forms of self-representation that foreground the collective. Particularly of interest are papers that interrogate the presumed unity of author, narrator, and protagonist; reconsider the autobiographical pact in light of collaborative and collective forms of narration; and explore how communities write themselves into history through shared acts of remembrance, testimony, and self-representation. Topics to consider include, but are not limited to: Collaborative and edited autobiographies Oral histories and interview-based life narratives Community and collective testimony Indigenous, Black, and diasporic collective life writing Gender, Sexuality and self testaments Archives, memory, and community self-representation Theoretical reconsiderations of the autobiographical pact and authorial voice Experimental and hybrid forms of autobiography Collective authorship, voice, and narrative authority Life writing, activism, and community formation. By centering collective modes of self-account, this panel seeks to expand the boundaries of autobiography studies and establish interventions that consider how communities, rather than individuals alone, become autobiographical subjects.

items.fullDescription: Call for Papers NeMLA's 58th Annual Convention - "Empowering Communities" March 6-9, 2027 | Salve Regina University, Newport, Rhode Island (Hybrid Conference) Panel Title: Tracing Female Body, Art & Economy in the Global South Primary Area / Secondary Area: Women's and Gender Studies / Interdisciplinary Humanities Modality: Hybrid Chair: Maryam Raza (University of Auckland, New Zealand) This panel explores how the historical treatment of female bodies in the Global South through the patriarchal structures of subjectification, commodification, sexualization and objectification has been reversed by contemporary strategies of resistance and reclamation. The panel examines how the postmodern woman in diverse socio-economic contexts "trains" the masculine gaze (Dixit 2024) over her body, renegotiating agency. Women increasingly reconfigure the female body through carnivalesque, alternative spaces of identity, creativity and skill through embodied forms. The female body evolves from a passive object of desire into an active, expressive, and economically productive site. Thus, women transform spaces of suppression to places of resistance and empowerment. Hussain et al. (2025) note that emancipation precedes autonomy and decision-making in socio-economic spheres. The panel further examines the intersection of bodily narratives with performative femininity, wellness cultures, fitness regimes, and beauty and anti-aging industries. It also restructures the domestic space as a site of culinary entrepreneurship, aided by digital platforms. Consequently, the Global South woman utilizes such arenas to enhance visibility, economic upward mobility, and global networks beyond what Fahmida Riaz termed the "Four Walled Homestead." Drawing on interdisciplinary approaches from cultural, gender, media, literary, and postcolonial studies, this panel foregrounds how women collectively construct bodily identities that both challenge and adapt to tradition. The selected papers will revolve around the following themes: Women and wellness culture Gender, food, and culinary skills Ageing Dance Cultures Women and Performing Arts Fetishizing and infantilizing women within beauty industry Submission Deadline : September 30, 2026 Please submit abstracts via the NeMLA portal: https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22411 For queries about the panel, prospective participants are warmly encouraged to contact the chair: Maryam Raza mraz655@aucklanduni.ac.nz

items.title: Literary and Cultural Communities Between Asia and the Hispanophone World
items.excerpt: From the early European missionaries who ventured into Asia all the way to the contemporary networks of connection that currently link Spain and Latin America countries like China, Japan and Korea, the cultural, literary and artistic links between Asia and the Hispanophone world run deep. items.fullDescription: From the early European missionaries who ventured into Asia all the way to the contemporary networks of connection that currently link Spain and Latin America countries like China, Japan and Korea, the cultural, literary and artistic links between Asia and the Hispanophone world run deep. Based on religious, linguistic, cultural, literary or artistic points of contact, numerous groups and communities have maintained active and productive lines of connection that have transform their own localities through the implementation of imported ideas, products, concepts from the other side of the world. Considering the long-standing lines of dialogue between these two vast geographic spaces, we invite scholars reflecting on questions such as: How Hispanophone writers and artists have searched in Asian literary practices spaces for renewal and innovation? How communities from Asian countries have assimilated and reinterpreted Latin American cultural products? How do the cultural products and creative practices of the two sides of the Pacific Ocean mirror/distort/reinvent each other? Papers dealing with literature, theatre, cinema, and other cultural productions are welcome. This is board-sponsored panel accepts presentations in English and Spanish are welcome.

items.fullDescription: This panel explores the intersections between contemporary South Korean and Italian women's writing through the lenses of memory, gender, trauma, migration, and post-national identity. While these literary traditions are often studied separately within distinct geopolitical and linguistic frameworks, the panel proposes a comparative and transnational approach that foregrounds shared concerns surrounding patriarchal structures, historical violence, intergenerational memory, and the negotiation of belonging. Bringing together scholars working on Italian and Korean women writers, the panel examines how literature becomes a space for articulating marginalized experiences and challenging dominant national narratives. The selected texts engage with themes such as family silences, colonial and postwar legacies, diaspora, racialization, feminist activism, labor, and the body as an archive of memory. Through memoir, autofiction, and experimental narrative forms, these writers construct alternative genealogies and forms of counter-memory that complicate homogeneous understandings of identity and nationhood. The panel seeks to foster an interdisciplinary conversation between Asian Studies, Italian Studies, Comparative Literature, Gender Studies, and Migration Studies. By placing South Korean and Italian women writers in dialogue, the session highlights the productive possibilities of comparative feminist methodologies across different cultural contexts while emphasizing shared strategies of resistance, care, and narrative reconstruction.

items.fullDescription: Language is key to communication, whether visual, auditory, linguistic, spatial, or gestural. In literature, modalities might include devices such as phraseology, tone, sensory elements, symbolism, satire, irony, characterizations, or figurative language. Modalities in literature can be narrative, descriptive, persuasive, expository, causal, effectual, definitive, comparative, and argumentative. Through close and careful examination of "voice," "word," or "non-verbal expressions" of speaker or writer, someone might discover surprising probabilities, poignant certainties, unexpected opportunities, awakened obligations, absurd necessities, or unusual permissions that shift from "a state of passivity to being active agents of change." This roundtable session will explore, define, determine, and discuss how key modalities in various literatures have the ability to excite, intrigue, and empower communities--large and small, academic and personal, collective and individual-to values of positivity and collectiveness.

items.fullDescription: This panel seeks papers on artistic movements, individuals or events that demonstrate how living as an outsider or in exile can facilitate cross-cultural learning and creating. The call is based on ideas of reterritorializing lost, outside, and minoritarian cultures such as the Négritude movement-which was recently celebrated at the center of the Paris-Noir exhibit at the Pompidou- and what Gertrude Stein calls the Lost Generation to find empowerment in exile. We seek to share techniques of research and teaching about the power of the powerless by studying overlooked artistic communities, individuals and cultures. For example we welcome submissions about how artists of the Paris Noir and/or Lost Generation relate to issues of culture, creating and community in our time. Many are unaware that James Baldwin, Miles Davis, and Jean-Michel Basquiat worked alongside Ernest Hemingway, Gertrude Stein and Henry Miller. These expatriates and African American artists electively inhabited the creative "Undercommons" of Paris Noir to remain generative despite fascism, racism, and war. These artists who were foreigners and outsiders are now classic and read inside the classroom by a majority in culture. Submit papers that analyze minorities, foreigners, strangers, exiles and outsiders in literature, film, or culture as a model for reflecting upon ways of reterritorializing the classroom and community. Below are some questions to orient your submission, although you are not limited to working on these: How does the marginalized turn the margin to a source of empowerment? What type of artist or work demonstrates change on the margins? What does it mean to inhabit to the role of foreigner, outsider, or minority? How does the existence of the stranger, immigrant, minority, or neighbor facilitate cross-cultural dialogue and generativity? How do we inhabit and engage vulnerability, process, child-like discovery, repetition and outside as a way of innovating that resists an authoritarian or artificial rule? Hybrid: The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included. All abstract submissions must be submitted through the NeMLA portal. https://cfplist.com/nemla/ Home/S/22549 Chair: Dr. Jessica Datema, jdatema@bergen.edu, https://bergen.edu/academics/academic-divisions-departments/english-department/our-composition-literature-faculty/jessica-datema/ https://www.nemla.org/convention.html

items.fullDescription: In the four hundred years since its invention in Renaissance Florence, opera has become synonymous with the grandiose, the excessive, and the melodramatic, yet it has only gained a foothold in the academy as an object of serious academic study within the past fifty years. Since then, however, an abundance of scholarship has yielded everything from formal musicological readings of operatic works to theoretical inquiries inspired by psychoanalysis into voice and performance. And topics like the relationship between opera and sovereignty in seventeenth century Italy and the appropriation of Wagner by the Third Reich underscore how opera has never been far from the political sphere in the Western world. But as Slavoj Zizek confirms, "the more opera is dead, the more it flourishes." Opera's story may be one of perpetual decadence, but contemporary creators deserving of academic attention push against the narrative and economic forces that have challenged the validity of opera as an artform in the 21st century. This panel seeks to complement contemporary scholarship on opera across the humanities by soliciting papers that speak to opera's unique, overdetermined, and intrinsically interdisciplinary blend of words, music, performance, and history, as well as papers that examine operatic genealogies extant in non-operatic texts. Potential panelists are also invited to think across languages, borders, and performance traditions and consider the important social and political questions of canonicity, representation, and ownership as they pertain to opera and the operatic.

items.fullDescription: Hybrid: The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included. This panel invites contributions that examine how food and foodways shape and reflect cultural knowledge across time and place, with particular attention to the relationships among foodways, memory, migration, identity, cultural heritage, and community formation. Proposals are welcome from a range of disciplinary and professional perspectives that explore food and cultural identity in literature, cinema, and other forms of media; oral history projects; mapping initiatives; community archives and digital collections; public-facing scholarship; and pedagogical approaches to Food Studies. The panel also invites discussion of emerging technologies and methodologies that offer new ways of researching, representing, and engaging with foodways and cultural heritage, while expanding opportunities for the communication and exchange of cultural knowledge. Whether through literary and cinematic representation, historical research, oral histories, or other forms of cultural production and inquiry, food-centred scholarship offers valuable insights into the ways individuals and communities understand and communicate culture across generations and geographies. In keeping with the conference theme, Empowering Communities, this session considers how foodways can serve as sites of cultural expression, knowledge-sharing, and collective memory, while encouraging reflection on questions of belonging, exchange, continuity, and cultural change.
